coredata.dc:title: Enhanced sorption and fluorescent detection of bisphenol A by using sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibrils/ZIF-8 composite hydrogel

Wang, Caichao
Yuan, Yao
Xie, Xiangjing

Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: To address the issue of bisphenol A (BPA) contamination in wastewater, a novel hydrogel, sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibrils/ZIF-8 composite hydrogel (SCZC), was synthesized for efficient BPA removal. The SCZC exhibited an exceptional adsorption capacity of 1696 mg/g, aligning well with both Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models. Furthermore, it exhibited remarkable regeneration properties, maintaining 89.1 % of its adsorption capacity even after undergoing five adsorption-desorption cycles. The synthesized SCZC also acted as a fluorescent sensor for detecting BPA, employing dynamic quenching and offering linear detection ranges of 10–100 mg/L and 0.2–1.0 μg/L, with a low detection limit of 0.06 μg/L. Analysis of adsorption and detection mechanisms revealed that SCZC's exceptional performance could be attributed to the three-dimensional (3D) porous structure formed by sodium alginate and cellulose nanofibrils. Economic analysis indicated that SCZC, in comparison to commercially activated carbon, was relatively inexpensive. This study introduces a novel approach for designing and preparing a sodium alginate-based hydrogel incorporating metal-organic frameworks, offering simultaneous BPA detection and removal capabilities.

head.ce:title: Enhanced sorption and fluorescent detection of bisphenol A by using sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibrils/ZIF-8 composite hydrogel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the issue of bisphenol A (BPA) contamination in wastewater, a novel hydrogel, sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibrils/ZIF-8 composite hydrogel (SCZC), was synthesized for efficient BPA removal. The SCZC exhibited an exceptional adsorption capacity of 1696 mg/g, aligning well with both Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models. Furthermore, it exhibited remarkable regeneration properties, maintaining 89.1 % of its adsorption capacity even after undergoing five adsorption-desorption cycles. The synthesized SCZC also acted as a fluorescent sensor for detecting BPA, employing dynamic quenching and offering linear detection ranges of 10–100 mg/L and 0.2–1.0 μg/L, with a low detection limit of 0.06 μg/L. Analysis of adsorption and detection mechanisms revealed that SCZC's exceptional performance could be attributed to the three-dimensional (3D) porous structure formed by sodium alginate and cellulose nanofibrils. Economic analysis indicated that SCZC, in comparison to commercially activated carbon, was relatively inexpensive. This study introduces a novel approach for designing and preparing a sodium alginate-based hydrogel incorporating metal-organic frameworks, offering simultaneous BPA detection and removal capabilities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is a chemical extensively utilized in the production of polycarbonate plastics and epoxy resins [1]. It poses significant risks to fetal and child development, hormone levels [2], and may lead to carcinogenic tumors, birth defects, and other developmental disorders [3]. Consequently, the efficient removal of BPA from wastewater has emerged as a critical concern. Various methods exist for BPA treatment, including advanced oxidation processes [4], biological treatment [5], photocatalytic degradation [6], membrane separation [7], and adsorption [8]. Among these, adsorption stands out due to its high efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and ease of control, making it a highly suitable technology for wastewater purification [9]. Given the emphasis on affordability and efficiency, the synthesis of environmentally friendly and effective adsorbents holds paramount importance in BPA treatment.

Hydrogels, well-known for their hydrophilic nature and three-dimensional (3D) porous structure, have garnered significant attention as adsorbents in wastewater treatment. Kono et al. [10] developed hydrogel microspheres specifically for BPA adsorption, utilizing carboxymethylcellulose sodium salt and β-cyclodextrin as raw materials, achieving a maximum adsorption capacity of 167 μmol/g. Among the raw materials in hydrogel synthesis, sodium alginate (SA), a naturally derived carbon material abundant in oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl), exhibits non-toxicity, biodegradability, excellent biocompatibility, and notable advantages [11]. SA-based hydrogels offer distinct benefits in heavy metal adsorption. For example, Wang et al. [12] employed a hydrogel formed through SA/polyvinyl alcohol polymerization to remove lead ions from wastewater. Zhao et al. [13] synthesized composite hydrogels using SA, polyethylene oxide, and nano-materials for the adsorption of Cu(II) and Cd(II) from wastewater. Additionally, Zhang et al. [14] utilized SA composite hydrogel to enhance Cu(II) adsorption in aqueous solutions. Furthermore, the oxygen-containing functional groups present in SA-based hydrogel can facilitate hydrogen bonding with BPA, indicating significant potential for efficient BPA adsorption.

Cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) are commonly manufactured by defibrillation of cellulose fibers through application of high shear forces to expose the fibers' substructural fibrils and microfibrils. CNFs exhibit outstanding characteristics, including a high specific surface area, unique hydrophilic properties, and the ability to readily form 3D porous structures [15]. Therefore, CNFs are promising candidates for preparing hydrogel to remove pollutants.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are crystalline materials composed of metal ions or clusters and organic ligands, featuring a network structure with highly adjustable pores and a vast specific surface area, conferring unique properties and significant application potential [16]. A notable MOF, Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), possesses a square natrium-type structure [17]. Studies by Yang et al. [18] and Khan et al. [19] have confirmed ZIF-8's efficiency as an adsorbent for removing pollutants from wastewater. However, ZIF-8 in powdered form tends to aggregate easily, hindering its practical application [20]. To address this issue, we propose incorporating ZIF-8 into a suitable matrix, such as an SA-based hydrogel.

The detection of BPA in aqueous media is of paramount importance. Traditional methods for BPA detection include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). However, these methods require expensive equipment, extended analytical time, skilled technicians, and intricate sample pretreatment, thereby limiting their practical application. In recent years, fluorescence analysis has emerged as a promising technique for accurately detecting BPA, offering advantages such as low sample requirement, cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, time efficiency, and ease of handling [21]. Rajesh et al. synthesized carbon dots (CDs)–vanadium pentoxide nanoporous material, demonstrating selective sensing of BPA within the concentration range of 5 × 10−9 M to 9.2 × 10−3 M [22]. Hwang et al. prepared CDs using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as a precursor, exhibiting high selectivity for BPA detection [23]. However, the practical application of CDs faces challenges due to their small interparticle distances, leading to easy aggregation. Loading CDs onto a carrier emerges as a viable solution to address these issues, with an SA-based hydrogel showing potential as a suitable carrier.

This study aims to utilize SA, CNFs, CDs, and ZIF-8 in the fabrication of SCZC for both the adsorption and detection of BPA. Within the SCZC structure, CNFs play a crucial role as the hydrogel “skeleton,” enhancing the specific surface area. CDs contribute by enhancing amino group-induced adsorption for BPA and serving as a fluorescence sensor, enabling both qualitative and quantitative detection. ZIF-8 provides additional sorption sites for BPA. Various analytical techniques are employed to investigate SCZC's properties, followed by an exploration of its adsorption and detection characteristics toward BPA. The potential mechanisms of adsorption and detection are also discussed, and a cost estimation for SCZC is finally conducted.

Bleached wood pulp (BWP) Shijiazhuang Lishang Mineral Products Processing Co., Ltd. (Hebei China). TEMPO (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical, 99 %), sodium alginate (SA, AR grade), sodium bromide (NaBr, AR grade), sodium hypochlorite pentahydrate (NaClO·5H2O, ≥39 %), citric acid monohydrate (AR grade), ethylenediamine monohydrate (98 %), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98 %), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AR grade), 2-methylimidazole (C4H6N2, 98 %), and calcium chloride (CaCl2, AR grade) were bought from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co, Ltd. (Shanghai China).

5.0 g of BWP were dispersed in 500 mL of ultrapure water using mechanical stirring for 30 min. 64 mg of TEMPO and 1.27 g of NaBr were dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water, and this solution was gradually added to the BWP suspension. 80 mL of NaClO were gradually added to the mixture. The resulting mixture was maintained at 25 °C for 2.5 h, with pH carefully controlled at 10. Subsequently, the mixture was thoroughly rinsed, and subjected to homogenization six times using a high-pressure homogenizer (Microfluidizer M-110 P, Microfluidics Corp., Newton, MA, U.S.A.) to produce CNFs. The CNFs were transferred into a dialysis bag and subjected to a 5-day dialysis procedure.

0.42 g of citric acid, 536 μL of ethylenediamine, and 10 mL of ultrapure water were combined. The mixture was then transferred to an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. Subsequently, the resulting CDs were cooled to 25 °C and stored for future use.

Various quantities of CDs (25 μL, 50 μL, 75 μL, 100 μL, 125 μL, and 150 μL), maintaining a constant concentration of 0.5 wt%, were combined with EDC (0.06 g) and NHS (0.23 g). This mixture was then added to a 10.0 g suspension of CNFs (2.0 wt%) and homogenized using 15 min of ultrasonic treatment for uniformity. A 3.5 pH acetic acid buffer (5 mL) was introduced into the mixture and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 25 °C for 24 h. Ultimately, CNFs-CDs-x (x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6) were prepared. Based on fluorescence intensity, CNFs-CDs-4 was selected (Fig. S1).

Dissolved 1.17 g of zinc nitrate in 8 mL of ultrapure water to create mixture 1. Combined 22.7 g of 2-methylimidazole with 80 mL of ultrapure water to form mixture 2. Mixture 1 was added to mixture 2 at 25 °C and stirred for 5 min. The resulting sample was then centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min, washed three times with ultrapure water, and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 h before being ground into a powder.

The orthogonal experimental design method was employed to analyze the impact of SA, CNFs-CDs, and ZIF-8 on the adsorption capacity (Qe) of the prepared hydrogel [24]. The orthogonal design table L 9 (34) was utilized, and the test program is presented in Table S1. The adsorption capacity of the prepared hydrogel was measured under specific conditions: initial solution pH (7), temperature (T, 25 °C), and initial concentration (C0, 60 mg/L). The optimal hydrogel, SCZC-5, was identified for achieving maximum Qe.

Typically, 10.0 g of SA solution (1.5 wt%), 2 g of CNFs-CDs, and 0.03 g of ZIF-8 were ultrasonically stirred for 10 min. The mixture was then injected into a CaCl2 solution (0.1 wt%) using a syringe pump and stirred at 15 rpm for 3 h. To ensure complete gelation, the target product, SCZC-5, was immersed in a CaCl2 solution at 4 °C for 10 h. Finally, SCZC-5 was thoroughly washed with ultrapure water three times to remove unreacted reagents. Varying the amount of CNFs-CDs from 0.4 g to 3.2 g, the synthesized hydrogels were labeled as SCZC-x (x = 1 to 8). Additionally, a hydrogel lacking ZIF-8 was named SCC-5, and another one, lacking both ZIF-8 and CNFs-CDs, was prepared and denoted as OS, serving as two controls.

In this study, a BPA solution was prepared using ethanol as a stock solvent (5000 mg/L) due to its limited solubility in water. It was subsequently diluted with water to achieve various BPA concentrations [25].

Investigated the batch adsorption of BPA on the prepared hydrogel, taking into account parameters such as T, pH, C0, and t. Specifically, 42 mg of the prepared hydrogel was added to a 200 mL solution of BPA with predetermined T, pH, and C0. The conical flask containing the mixture was agitated in a thermostatic shaker set at 150 rpm. Investigated the influence of T ranging from 15 °C to 55 °C on BPA adsorption using a 60 mg/L BPA solution, while maintaining a constant initial pH of 7, over a duration of 225 min at 25 °C. Emphasized the influence of pH on adsorption performance, indicating the need for further investigation. At 25 °C, 42 mg of the prepared hydrogel was added to 200 mL of BPA solutions with a C0 of 60 mg/L. The pH was adjusted to values ranging from 3 to 11 using 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH solutions. The adsorption isotherm study for BPA removal was conducted at 25 °C, utilizing 42 mg of the prepared hydrogels in 200 mL solutions with C0 ranging from 20 to 5000 mg/L. Adsorption kinetic data were obtained by conducting experiments at 25 °C, varying the t from 0 to 225 min, while maintaining a constant T of 25 °C and a pH of 7.

To study the reusability, multiple adsorption and desorption tests were performed. SCZC-5 was effortlessly extracted from the reaction vessel without filtration or centrifugation. Subsequently, SCZC-5 was immersed in anhydrous ethanol for 5 h. Following elution, SCZC-5 was rinsed thrice with deionized water. The desorbed SCZC-5 was then employed for another adsorption-desorption cycle.

The micromorphology of the fabricated samples was observed using a JEM-1400 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-1400, JEOL Ltd., Japan) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8010, Hitachi, Japan), respectively. For chemical functional group analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was conducted with a Bruker VERTEX 70 instrument from Germany, covering the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 with a resolution of 4cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted utilizing a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer, employing Cu Kα irradiation, a 40-mA current, and a 40 kV voltage to determine the crystal structures of the fabricated samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a Shimadzu Axis Ultra DLD instrument to analyze the surface chemical states and composition of the samples. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Hitachi High-Technology Co., Japan. The surface area of the fabricated samples was determined using a BET surface area analyzer (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics, Norcross, GA).

The process of synthesizing SCZC is illustrated in Fig. 1 . SCZC consists of four main components: SA, ZIF-8, CNFs, and CDs. Initially, the synthesis of CNFs-CDs involved grafting CDs onto CNFs. Subsequently, the CNFs-CDs were meticulously blended with SA and ZIF-8. Next, calcium ions (Ca2+) were introduced. The synthesis process concluded with the formation of the final product: a 3D porous structured SCZC.

SEM images illustrating CNFs, CNFs-CDs, ZIF-8, and SCZC-5 were provided. CNFs displayed a smooth fibrillar structure (Fig. 2a), while CDs exhibited a nanosized spherical structure (Fig. S2). The grafting of CDs onto CNFs induced a roughening effect on the surface of CNFs-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 2b. ZIF-8 appeared as plates with an average size of 50 nm (Fig. 2c). SCZC-5 exhibited a 3D porous structure, as observed in Fig. 2d. EDS analyses were performed to determine elemental distribution in SCZC-5, as illustrated in Fig. S3. It was observed that CNFs-CDs and ZIF-8 were homogeneously distributed throughout SCZC-5.

XRD patterns of SA, CNFs, CNFs-CDs, ZIF-8, and SCZC-5 are illustrated in Fig. 2e. SA showed two peaks at 13.2° and 21.5°, attributed to its semi-crystalline structure [26], as identified in the JCPDS card reference (JCPDS card no. 00-069-1523). CNFs and CNFs-CDs exhibited peaks at 16.8° and 21.8°, corresponding to the (11’0) and (200) crystal faces of cellulose [27], as identified in the JCPDS card reference (JCPDS card no. 00-050-2241). ZIF-8 exhibited eight peaks at 7.3° (011), 10.4° (002), 12.8° (112), 14.8° (022), 16.5° (013), 18.3° (222), 24.6° (233), and 26.7° (134) [28], in accordance with the JCPDS card reference (JCPDS card no. 00-062-1030). For SCZC-5, the aforementioned characteristic peaks exhibited varying characteristics. The peaks at 13.2° and 21.5° from SA were almost identical, suggesting that the crosslinking reaction of SA in SCZC-5 had minimal impact on the crystal structure of SA. Peaks at 16.8° and 21.8° associated with CFNs-CDs showed a slight decrease in intensity, possibly due to hydrogen bonding between CFNs-CDs and SA, impacting some of the crystal structure of CFNs-CDs [29]. The eight peaks associated with ZIF-8 were not visible, possibly due to the construction of a 3D porous structure in the SCZC-5 that wrapped or shrouded the ZIF-8 crystals.

Functional groups of SA, CNFs, CNFs-CDs, ZIF-8, and SCZC-5 were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2f). SA exhibited two peaks at 1608cm−1 and 1021cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–O–O and C–O–C, respectively [30]. A prominent peak at 1053 cm−1 in CNFs and CNFs-CDs indicated C − O − C stretching vibration [31]. Additionally, CNFs-CDs exhibited a new peak at 1650cm−1, indicating the stretching vibration of –CO–NH–, confirming crosslinking between the CDs and the CNFs [32]. In FTIR spectrum of ZIF-8, an additional peak at 990–1424 cm−1 was associated with CN stretching vibration, while the peak at 1587 cm−1 resulted from CN stretching vibration [33]. Regarding SCZC-5, the main characteristic peaks exhibited variations. The carboxylate peak (–COO−) from SA shifted to a lower wavenumber, likely due to the combination of –COO− and Ca2+, indicating successful formation of calcium-crosslinked SCZC-5 [34]. Peaks associated with ZIF-8 were not clearly discernible, possibly because of its incorporation of ZIF-8 into the 3D porous structure of SCZC-5. Additionally, the characteristic band at 3340 cm−1 from CNFs-CDs shifted to a lower wavenumber, indicating the connection between CNFs-CDs and SA network through hydrogen bonding.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were used to determine the specific surface area of the prepared samples (Fig. S4). The BET specific surface areas of SA, CNFs, and CNFs-CDs were 1.51, 4.56, and 5.64 m2/g, respectively. The BET specific surface area of CNFs-CDs was slightly larger than that of CNFs, attributed to the grafting of CDs onto CNFs [35]. ZIF-8 exhibited a significant BET specific surface area, suggesting its potential to enhance SA-based hydrogels. Notably, SCZC-5 demonstrated a substantially higher BET specific surface area of 242.17 m2/g, attributed to its 3D porous structure and incorporation of ZIF-8.

XPS was utilized to analyze the composition of SCZC-5 (Fig. S5). The XPS survey spectrum revealed the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), zinc (Zn), sodium (Na), and calcium (Ca). The presence of Na (a characteristic element of SA) and Zn (a characteristic element of ZIF-8) confirmed the incorporation of ZIF-8 into the SA network of SCZC-5. Furthermore, the detection of Ca provided strong evidence for the formation of a calcium-crosslinked SA network in SCZC-5.

The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of SCZC-5 were analyzed to characterize its optical properties. Fig. 2 g illustrates characteristic absorption peaks of SCZC-5 at 268 nm and 360 nm. The PL emission spectrum of SCZC-5, as shown in Fig. 2 h, demonstrated excitation-independence. This observation confirmed the uniform particle size of CDs rather than surface states, consistent with prior findings [36].

Fig. 2i illustrates the color changes of SCZC-x under visible and UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm. These color variations may stem from differences in CDs content. Particularly, among the SCZC samples, SCZC-5 exhibited the brightest fluorescence, with an absolute quantum yield (AQY0) calculated as 22.93 %.

Optimization of BPA adsorption was conducted using response surface methodology [37]. Three key factors—pH, T, and C0—were systematically considered, as detailed in Table S2. The experimental design matrix included 17 runs, outlined in Table S3. Comprehensive results of the analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the F-test are provided in Table S4. These analyses confirmed the significance of pH, T, and C0 in influencing the Qe of SCZC-5. As shown in Fig. 3a-c, the interaction between pairs of factors was effectively visualized.

Fig. 3d illustrates the impact of pH on BPA uptake. Initially, the Qe of SCZC-5 increased and then decreased, reaching its maximum value at pH 7. The deprotonation constant of BPA ranges from 9.6 to 10.2 [38]. This variation in Qe can be explained as follows: when the pH was below 7.0, BPA remained in its molecular form and did not dissociate. However, when the pH exceeded 7, deprotonation of BPA took place, resulting in the formation of anionic species such as BPA− or BPA2− [39]. Additionally, deprotonation of the –COOH and –OH groups of SCZC-5 occurred simultaneously, causing the material to become ionized and consequently reducing its adsorption capacity [38]. Consequently, a pH of 7 was chosen for further experiments.

Temperature significantly affects the adsorption properties of SCZC-5. Fig. 3e shows that the Qe of SCZC-5 for BPA decreased as the temperature increased from 15 °C to 55 °C. The confirmation of the exothermic nature of the adsorption process was evident from the data. The thermodynamic parameters including ΔG, ΔH, and ΔS, were calculated using the temperature-dependent sorption equation and Van't Hoff equation (Fig. S6), as described in the Supplementary Material. Negative ΔG values at various temperatures indicated the spontaneous adsorption of BPA by SCZC-5. Furthermore, negative ΔH value (−22.92 kJ/mol) indicated that the adsorption process was exothermic. Negative ΔS values suggested an increase in disorder during the adsorption process [40].

Fig. 3f illustrates the adsorption isotherms of BPA on OS, SCC-5, and SCZC-5. Specifically, the equilibrium points of these three samples increased with rising C0, reaching equilibrium thereafter. At identical C0, SCZC-5 demonstrated a higher Qe compared to OS and SCC-5, indicating that the incorporation of CNFs-CDs and ZIF-8 improved BPA adsorption [41]. The Langmuir and Freundlich models were employed to analyze BPA adsorption by SCZC-5, as shown in Fig. 3 h-i. The coefficient of determination (R2) values for the Langmuir and Freundlich models were 0.982 and 0.964, respectively. The Langmuir model, as demonstrated in Table S5, exhibited a superior fit with the experimental data, with a maximum adsorption capacity (Qmax) reached 1696 mg/g, indicating a monolayer adsorption process [42]. Additionally, Table 1 compares the BPA adsorption capacity of SCZC-5 with adsorbents reported in the literature, highlighting the efficacy of SCZC-5 as an absorbent.

The investigation of adsorption kinetics aimed to elucidate the relationship between adsorption amount (Qt) and t. As illustrated in Fig. 3 g, with C0 set at 60 mg/L, all three samples rapidly adsorbed BPA until reaching equilibrium. At the same t, SCZC-5 exhibited a larger Qt than OS and SCC-5, highlighting the beneficial role of CNFs-CDs and ZIF-8 in BPA adsorption. The analysis of the experimental data involved the application of pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) models, as shown in Fig. 3j-k. The R2 values for the PFO and PSO models were 0.984 and 0.994, respectively. As shown in Table S6, the PSO model demonstrated a better fit, suggesting that chemisorption may be the dominant step limiting the rate.

Anhydrous ethanol was employed as the solvent for the desorption of BPA from the adsorbents. Fig. 4a illustrates five consecutive adsorption-desorption cycles of SCZC-5. SCZC-5 maintained an adsorptive capacity of approximately 89.1 % even after five cycles. The SEM image (Fig. S7) revealed that the structural morphology of SCZC-5 after five adsorption-desorption cycles closely resembled that of the as-synthesized SCZC-5, indicating the retention of its 3D porous structure [54]. The FTIR and XRD patterns of reused SCZC-5, as depicted in Fig. 4b and c, closely resembled those of the as-synthesized SCZC-5. These findings offered conclusive evidence of the excellent stability of the prepared SCZC-5.

SCZC-5's adsorption capability was evaluated not only in ultrapure water but also in tap water and lake water obtained from the campus of Central South University of Forestry and Technology in Changsha, China. Samples underwent filtration using Whatman filter paper 40 to remove soil particles. Water quality parameters were documented in Table S7. SCZC-5 exhibited Qe values of 25.4 mg/g and 32.9 mg/g in tap water and lake water, respectively. These values were lower than the Qe achieved in ultrapure water, which was 53.7 mg/g. This decrease was attributed to the competition of other organic components present in tap water and lake water with BPA.

SCZC-5's selectivity for BPA was evaluated by introducing five structurally similar compounds (BPA, Tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA), Hydroquinone (HQ), 2,4-Dinitrophenol (2-NP), and phenyl hydroxide (PhOH)) to SCZC-5, each at a concentration of 200 mg/L, and observing fluorescence responses. Fig. 5a illustrates that BPA induced significant fluorescence quenching of SCZC-5, whereas the other compounds minimally or insignificantly affected fluorescence quenching. This change was quantified as the ratio of AQY to AQY0, where AQY represents the absolute quantum yield of SCZC-5 with the above compounds adsorbed, as depicted in Fig. 5b. The results clearly indicated that SCZC-5 exhibited high selectivity for BPA.

In order to achieve sensitive detection of BPA, optimization of various conditions such as T, t, and pH was carried out [55], as depicted in Fig. S8. Subsequently, detection experiments were performed at a T of 25 °C, a t of 3 min, and a pH of 7.

SCZC-5's sensitivity in detecting BPA was evaluated by exposing it to varying concentrations of BPA under optimal conditions. Fig. 6a depicts a strong linear relationship between the AQY and different BPA concentrations ranging from 10 to 100 mg/L. The resulting linear regression equation was determined as AQY = −0.07371 × C0 + 23.08, with an R2 value of 0.9914. The limit of detection (LOD), calculated using the equation 3σ/k, was determined to be 3.2 mg/L. Additionally, the fluorescence emission intensity of SCZC-5 decreased with increasing concentrations of BPA, as shown in Fig. 6b. The change in fluorescence emission intensity was fitted using the equation: I0/I-1 = 1.120 × C0 + 0.1630, where I0 represented the initial fluorescence intensity of SCZC-5, and I represented the fluorescence intensity of SCZC-5 in the presence of BPA. The linear equation was applicable in the range from 0.2 to 1.0 μg/L, with an R2 of 0.9933. The LOD was determined to be 0.06 μg/L, which was also calculated based on 3σ/k. Furthermore, compared to other references (refer to Table 2 ), SCZC-5 exhibited high sensitivity in detecting BPA as a fluorescent sensor.

SCZC-5's applicability was assessed by collecting two water samples from the campus of Central South University of Forestry and Technology (Changsha, China), for BPA determination. We conducted spiked tests by adding appropriate amounts of BPA to the water samples and analyzed them using SCZC-5. The results (refer to Table S8) showed recoveries ranging from 99.40 % to 103.09 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging between 0.25 % and 2.49 %. These results confirmed SCZC-5's applicability to real water samples.

To investigate the mechanism of BPA adsorption by SCZC-5, we performed FTIR and XPS analyses for SCZC-5. As shown in Fig. 7a, after BPA adsorption, a significant decrease in the O−H and N−H stretching signals at 3340 cm−1 was observed [68], suggesting hydrogen bonding between the protonated amino and hydroxyl groups of SCZC-5 and BPA. Moreover, a shift in the CO group from 1400 to 1420 cm−1 implied the presence of π-π interaction.

In Fig. 7b, the elemental composition of SCZC-5 was displayed, indicating the presence of Zn, O, N, Ca, and C. The O1s spectrum in Fig. 7c reveals two distinct peaks at 532.1 and 533.2 eV, attributed to –COOH and –OH, respectively [69]. Post-adsorption, –COOH shifted to 532.0 eV, indicating the formation of hydrogel bonding between –COOH and –OH of BPA [70]. In Fig. 7d, the N1s spectrum, ranging from 397 to 402 eV, exhibited three peaks: –N = at 398.7 eV, –NH2 at 400.0 eV, and –NH3 + at 400.9 eV [71]. Following adsorption, –NH2 shifted to 400.1 eV, indicating hydrogen bonding between SCZC-5 and BPA [72]. Additionally, –NH3 + shifted from 400.9 to 401.1 eV, suggesting cation-π interaction between –NH3 + and aromatic structures of BPA [73]. Fig. 7e depicted the C1s spectrum, showing characteristic peaks attributed to CO (288.2 eV), C–O/C–N (286.2 eV), and CC (284.8 eV) [74]. Notably, these peaks shifted to lower binding energy, potentially indicating the presence of π-π interaction. Overall, these findings confirmed that BPA interacted with SCZC-5 through hydrogen bonding, cation-π interaction, and π-π interaction.

To unravel the mechanism behind the fluorescence quenching of SCZC-5 induced by BPA, we conducted a comprehensive set of measurements, encompassing UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of BPA did not coincide with the fluorescence excitation peak of SCZC-5, as illustrated in Fig. 8a. Consequently, it was demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching of SCZC-5 was not attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or the inner filter effect (IFE). FRET depends on the spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission band of a fluorophore and the absorption band of an acceptor, whereas IFE refers to the absorption of excitation and emission of a fluorophore, leading to fluorescence attenuation. Hence, both FRET and IFE were ruled out. The quenching mechanism might involve photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [56]. Furthermore, Fig. 8b demonstrates that the fluorescence decay curves of SCZC-5 remain largely unchanged with and without BPA. The determined fluorescence lifetimes were 15.04 and 14.92 ns, respectively, indicating that the quenching process involved static quenching (SQ) rather than dynamic quenching [75]. In summary, these findings suggested that the quenching mechanism involved SQ accompanied by PET.

Fig. 9 elucidates the mechanism of BPA adsorption and detection by SCZC. The presence of amino and hydroxyl groups on SCZC's surface facilitates BPA enrichment through hydrogen bonding and cation-π interaction. Subsequently, BPA permeates the 3D porous structure and enters SCZC's interior. During diffusion, a fraction of BPA is adsorbed via π-π interaction with the benzene ring on the ZIF-8 backbone in SCZC. CNFs establish the hydrogel “skeleton,” while ZIF-8 and CDs loading introduces additional sorption sites, collectively enhancing SCZC's adsorption capacity. Additionally, CDs function as a fluorescence sensor for BPA detection. Incorporating CDs into SCZC's 3D porous structure further enhances the stability of CDs.

The cost significantly influences the practical application of adsorbents [76]. Thus, we conducted an analysis of the impact of SCZC-5 production costs on economic indicators (Table S9). The production cost of SCZC-5 was approximately 4.76 $/kg, representing merely 14.70 % of the cost of commercial activated carbon (32.39 $/kg). Given SCZC-5's Qmax value of 1696 mg/g, the cost of BPA removal was notably low, at just 0.003 $/g (refer to Table S10). In comparison, the cost of commercial activated carbon for BPA removal was significantly higher, at 0.343 $/g. Thus, SCZC-5 proved to be a more cost-effective adsorbent for BPA removal.

An innovative and efficient SCZC was synthesized for the simultaneous detection and adsorption of BPA. SCZC exhibited a high adsorption capacity of 1696 mg/g. Kinetic studies indicated that the adsorption followed a pseudo-second-order model, and the Langmuir model showed good agreement with the experimental adsorption isotherm. The relatively high adsorption efficiency of SCZC was attributed to the synergistic effect of CNFs, CDs, and ZIF-8. XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed that the adsorption mechanism of SCZC involved hydrogen bonding, cation-π interaction, and π-π interaction. SCZC-5 served as a sensitive probe for fluorescence sensing of BPA with a low LOD of 0.06 μg/L and linear ranges of 0.2–1.0 μg/L and 10–100 mg/L. Spiked recoveries ranging from 99.40 % to 103.09 %, with RSDs ranging from 0.25 % to 2.49 %, indicated that SCZC-5 was feasible for practical applications. Economic analysis further confirmed that the cost of SCZC-5 for BPA removal was significantly lower than that of commercialized activated carbon. Consequently, SCZC was developed as a cost-effective and reusable SA-based hydrogel for simultaneous detection and removal of BPA.
